上周，我的一位同事通知我，“推文”这个词现在包含在牛津英语词典中（见页面末尾的“安静公告”。）
 名词和动词推文（在社交网络中）刚刚添加到OED中。这突破了至少一个od规则，即新词需要在考虑到包含前十年的当前。但它似乎正在捕捉。
 这不是每天都在那个你帮助创建的词被添加到这个着名的出版物中，所以我以为我会分享一些“推文”这个词的早期历史。
 在Twitter的早期，服务使用“Twittering”作为动词和“Twitter-ers”作为名词。 2006年12月通讯是在回来的Lingo的一个很好的例子：
 来自：＆＃34;商业石头＆＃34;日期：2006年12月06日07:51:31 +1100主题：Twitter现在支持瞄准和更多你好Twitter-ers！这些天在Twitter总部真正升温了。上个月，推特用户的数量加倍，它看起来像它在12月份耗尽之前再次加倍。我们认为对所有这些积极活动作出反应的最佳方式是继续改进。所以通过并查看http://twitter.com！......这是现在的，快乐的推特！商业石头和Twitter团队http://twitter.com/biz.
 我开始在12月初开始使用Twitter。就像John Gruber和我的同事在图标，我喜欢我们的新的“互联网水冷却器”。但是，我是通过网站或仪表板小部件使用Twitter的不满意。
 在12月中旬淋浴时，一个想法袭击了我：将Twitter的新API联系起来给Cocoa网络类并展示带有推文的表。所以我干了一下开始原型：第二天我有世界上的第一个在我的Mac上运行的推特客户端。 
几天后，我在我们的存储库中检查了所有代码，Twitterrific出生了：
 R174 |克雷格| 2006-12-20 17:54:11 -0800（2006年12月20日星期三）| 1行初始导入
  当我开始实施用户界面时，很明显需要名词和动词。具有“Twitter更新”等标签的菜单项既是冗长和无聊。作为喜欢语言的人，使用像“刷新叽叽喳喳”这样的短语，让我的脖子后面的毛发站起来。
 所以我们开始称他们为“Twits”;更像是占位符。 2007年1月使用该术语感到像今天一样尴尬：
 @Dan：当你的Twit弹出时，我的妻子正在使用Mac，其次是“有一个名叫丹的人寻找爱情”。经典的。
 （感谢Jordan Kay在Twitter上挖掘那个推文。此外，“丹”是Dan Benjamin  - 我们在那些早期的日子里，我们没有想出屏幕名称，据我所知，Twitter只有一只丹！）
 幸运的是，事情即将改变。在1月3日，我在临时应用程序图标中检查。我总是把一些糟糕的东西作为我合作的才华横溢的人的灵感： 
R215 |克雷格| 2007-01-03 13:46:11 -0800（2007年1月3日星期三）| 1行添加了临时应用程序图标。更新的首选项面板（带QUIT和INFO）在窗口大小上调整。将扩展的大小添加到偏好。
    R225 |克雷格| 2007-01-04 13:21:01 -0800（星期四，2007年1月4日）| 2行添加了新的应用程序图标
 我们当时不知道它，但这也是一只鸟成为Twitter的代名词的时刻。在此时间点之前，Twitter的唯一身份是标识。
 在2007年1月的第一周，工作正在进行一场非常快速的速度。博格达发布经常和广泛分布。幸运的是，Twitter的人们正在使用我们的应用程序与其Snazzy新鸟图标。我们的一个测试人员是一个名叫布莱恩库克的API工程师，他们发给我以下电子邮件：
 来自：＆＃34; Blaine Cook＆＃34; ＆lt; [删除]＆gt;到：＆＃34;克雷格Hockenberry＆＃34; ＆lt; [删除]＆gt;日期：THU，2007年1月11日18:29:01 -0800主题：Twitterific.嘿克雷格，我用杰克的推特工作，只想用你来：Twitterific。首先，伟大的工作 - 我喜欢它，我认为办公室里也是如此！ :-)两个想法;首先，如何改变和＃34; Twit＆＃34; ＆＃34; Tweet＆＃34; - ＆＃34;官方名词＆＃34;是＆＃34; Twitter更新＆＃34;但是，无聊......？第二，I＆＃39; m在建立真实（Re：Flickr-style）身份验证API的过程中，使您能够为用户获得令牌，而无需存储其用户名和密码。我不希望其待处理的发布来停止您的开发或发布日期，因为我们将继续支持现有客户的基本职位，但您对测试API Macinations的Beta-Testing感兴趣吗？再次感谢Twitterific！布莱恩。
 （回想起来，这封电子邮件也是我们拼写应用程序名称的第一个提示！）
 当在软件中命名事物时，难以一致的协议，但在这种情况下，每个人都喜欢“推文”这个词。我很快更新了应用程序和网站： 
R263 |克雷格| 2007-01-12 11:46:22 -0800（星期五，2007年1月12日）| 1行改变＆＃34; Twits＆＃34; ＆＃34;推文＆＃34;删除了Twitterrific网页的自动启动。在配置窗口中添加了链接到Twitterrific网页。
 R2183 |克雷格| 2007-01-12 11:44:07 -0800（星期五，2007年1月12日）| 1行改变＆＃34; Twits＆＃34; ＆＃34;推文＆＃34;
 而不是太快了！我们只在产品网站上发布了三天后发布了第一个版本的TwitterRific：
 Twitterrific是一个有趣的小应用程序，可让您阅读和发布帖子或“推文”到Twitter社区网站。应用程序的用户界面清洁，简洁，旨在占用Mac桌面上的最少房地产。
 这是第一次“Tweet”在互联网上出现，参考你在Twitter上做的事情。
 我们的新应用程序是随处用户击中。我们甚至在Twitter的下一个简报中提到了：
 来自：＆＃34;商业石头＆＃34;日期：2007年1月17日星期三07:37:07 +1100主题：=在推特上升温你好Twitter-ers！......持续热情：推特在图标工厂中的人们已经创建了一个出色的小应用程序，可让您读取和发布到Twitter。应用程序＆＃39; S用户界面是干净的，简洁的，旨在占据Mac＆＃39; s桌面上的最少房地产。 Twitter总部正在房子里的每一个显示器上都会体育这个小型工作机会！从图标工厂网站免费下载它。Twitterrific：http：//iconfactory.com/software/twitterfrific.......事情正在升温！-biz stone和twitter团队http://twitter.com/biz. 
不幸的是，我从我们的应用程序开始“Twit”的工作并不完整：我忘记从某些按钮工具提示删除它。 1月25日版本1.1发布后，“Twit”已经消失了。
 即使使用“Tweet”一词迅速传播，Twitter需要一段时间来拥抱新的术语。即使经过几个月，“Twitter-ers”仍然“推动”。
 据我所知，第一次“推文”出现在Twitter的网站上是他们提到我们产品的时候。 （是的，Twitter的创始人会发送我们的功能建议时有一点时间点！）
 差不多一年后，“推文”是公司博客（但再次，在第三方的报价中）。
 在2008年6月，Twitter的博客上没有出现“Tweet”这个词。巧合，它是参考WWDC，我们赢得了Twitterrific的苹果设计奖！
 听到我帮助在媒体中一遍又一遍地提到的单词仍然感到奇怪。这是一个伟大的词语，与一家伟大的服务一起，到底，我很高兴我们不互相打电话！
  我通过我的Twitter档案挖了一下，并提出了一些有趣的推文，展示了第一个Twitter客户的如何以及何时。 
Twitter不属于2006年12月5日发生的第一个概念（在Twitterrifice存储库首次办理登机手续前几周）。）这一思维行受到了John Gruber发布的推文他正在使用的仪表板小部件：
   正在思考推特ui属于桌面，而不是小部件 - 它＆＃39;＆＃39;除非你＆＃39; re f12＆＃39; ed
   （请注意，在Twitter的早期，该网站是在@杰克的原始发布状态消息的原始想法上设计的。在帖子中暗示了用户名，因此“Craig Hockenberry”“正在尝试”。）
 到12月20日，我在项目的状态下感到充满信心去公众（也是原型代码被检查到存储库的日子）：
  在图标，每个人都会得到两周的圣诞休息时间。我宁愿代码代购:-)
  与许多产品不同，我们知道我们在早期建造的名称：
    这是第一个带有源参数的推文（告诉Twitter，客户端发布的推文。）我们使用Twitter Engineering来实现这一点，并且长期以来，Twitterfific是除“Web”之外的唯一来源。请注意，发布前两天就发生了！
note_en:
Last week, one of my colleagues informed me that the word “tweet” was  now included in the  Oxford English Dictionary (see “Quiet announcement” at the end of the page.)
 The noun and verb  tweet (in the social-networking sense) has just been added to the OED. This breaks at least one OED rule, namely that a new word needs to be current for ten years before consideration for inclusion. But it seems to be catching on.
 It’s not everyday that a word you helped create gets added to this prestigious publication, so I thought I’d share a bit of the early history of the word “tweet.”
 In the early days of Twitter, the service used “twittering” as a verb and “twitter-ers” as a noun. The  December 2006 newsletter is a great example of the lingo that was in use back then:
 From: "Biz Stone"   Date: Wed, 06 Dec 2006 07:51:31 +1100Subject: Twitter Now Supports AIM and MoreHello Twitter-ers!Things are really heating up at Twitter headquarters these days. Last month, the number of Twitter users doubled and it looks like it's going to double again before we run out of December. We thought the best way to react to all this positive activity would be to keep on making improvements. So come on by and check out http://twitter.com!…That's it for now, Happy Twittering!Biz Stone and The Twitter Teamhttp://twitter.com/biz
 I started  started using Twitter at the beginning of December. Like John Gruber and my colleagues at the Iconfactory, I loved our new “water cooler for the Internet.” I was, however, unhappy with using Twitter via the website or Dashboard widgets.
 While  taking a shower in the middle of December, an idea struck me: it wouldn’t be hard to hook up Twitter’s  new API to the Cocoa networking classes and display a table with tweets. So I dried off and started prototyping: the next day I had the world’s first Twitter client running on my Mac.
 A few days later, I checked all my code into our repository and  Twitterrific was born:
 r174 | craig | 2006-12-20 17:54:11 -0800 (Wed, 20 Dec 2006) | 1 lineInitial import
  As I started to implement the user interface, it was clear that nouns and verbs were needed. Menu items with labels like “Post a Twitter Update” were both wordy and boring. And as someone who loves language, using a phrase like “Refresh Twitterings” made the hairs on the back of my neck stand up.
 So we started calling them “twits”; more as a placeholder than anything.  Using the term in January 2007 felt just as awkward as it does today:
 @Dan: my wife was using the Mac when your twit popped up, followed by “there’s a guy named Dan who’s looking for love”. Classic.
 (Thanks to Jordan Kay at Twitter for  digging that tweet up. Also, the “Dan” is  Dan Benjamin—in those early days we hadn’t figured out screen names, and as far as I knew, there was only one Dan on Twitter!)
 Luckily, things were about to change. On the 3rd of January, I checked in a temporary application icon. I always check in something crappy as an inspiration for the  talented folks I work with:
 r215 | craig | 2007-01-03 13:46:11 -0800 (Wed, 03 Jan 2007) | 1 lineAdded a temporary application icon. Updated preferences panel (with quit and about info.) Tweaked on window size. Added expanded size to preferences.
    r225 | craig | 2007-01-04 13:21:01 -0800 (Thu, 04 Jan 2007) | 2 linesAdded new app icon
 We didn’t know it at the time, but this is also the moment when a bird became synonymous with Twitter. Prior to that point in time,  Twitter’s only identity was a logotype.
 Work was proceeding at a very fast pace during the first week of January 2007. Beta releases were frequent and widely distributed. Fortunately, the folks at Twitter were using our app with its snazzy new bird icon. One of our beta testers was an API engineer named  Blaine Cook who sent me the following email:
 From: "Blaine Cook" <[REDACTED]>To: "Craig Hockenberry" <[REDACTED]>Date: Thu, 11 Jan 2007 18:29:01 -0800Subject: TwitterificHey Craig,I work on Twitter with Jack, and just wanted to ping you re: Twitterific. First off, great work - I love it, and I think so does everyone else in the office! :-)Two thoughts; first, how about changing "twit" to "tweet" - the "official noun" is "Twitter Update", but that's boring...? Second, I'm in the process of building a real (re: flickr-style) authentication API, so that you'll be able to obtain tokens for users without having to store their usernames and passwords. I don't want its pending release to stall your development or release date, since we will continue to support basic auth for existing clients, but would you be interested in beta-testing the api machinations? Thanks again for Twitterific!blaine.
 (In retrospect, this email is also the first hint that we spelled the app’s name wrong!)
 It’s rare to have unanimous agreement when naming things in software, but in this case  everyone loved the word “tweet”. I quickly updated both the app and the website:
 r263 | craig | 2007-01-12 11:46:22 -0800 (Fri, 12 Jan 2007) | 1 lineChanged "twits" to "tweets". Removed auto launch of Twitterrific web page. Added link to Twitterrific web page in config window.
 r2183 | craig | 2007-01-12 11:44:07 -0800 (Fri, 12 Jan 2007) | 1 lineChanged "twits" to "tweets"
 And not a moment too soon! We  released the first version of Twitterrific just three days later with the following copy on the  product website:
 Twitterrific is a fun little application that lets you both read and publish posts or “tweets” to the  Twitter community website. The application’s user interface is clean, concise and designed to take up a minimum of real estate on your Mac’s desktop.
 This is the first time “tweet” appeared on the Internet in reference to something you do on Twitter.
 Our new app was a hit with users everywhere. We even got a mention in Twitter’s  next newsletter:
 From: "Biz Stone"   Date: Wed, 17 Jan 2007 07:37:07 +1100Subject: =Things Are Heating Up At TwitterHello Twitter-ers!…Continued Hotness: TwitterrificThe folks over at The Icon Factory have created a brilliant little application that lets you both read and post updates to Twitter. The application's user interface is clean, concise and designed to take up a minimum of real estate on your Mac's desktop. Twitter headquarters is sporting this little jobbie on every monitor in the house! Download it for free from The Icon Factory web site.Twitterrific:  http://iconfactory.com/software/twitterrific…Things are heating up!-Biz Stone and The Twitter Teamhttp://twitter.com/biz
 Unfortunately, the work I had done to expunge “twit” from our app was not complete: I had forgotten to remove it from some button tooltips. After the  release of version 1.1 on January 25th, “twit” was gone for good.
 Even though the use of the word “tweet” spread quickly, Twitter took awhile to embrace the new terminology. Even after several months,  “twitter-ers” were still “twittering”.
 As far as I know, the first time “tweet” appeared on Twitter’s site was when  they referred to our product. (And yes, there was a point in time when the  founder of Twitter would send us feature suggestions!)
 Almost a year later, “tweet” was  used on the company blog (but again, in a quote by a third party).
 The word “tweet” didn’t appear unquoted on Twitter’s blog until  June of 2008. Coincidentally, it was in reference to the WWDC where we  won an Apple Design Award for Twitterrific!
 It still feels strange to hear a word I helped create be mentioned over and over again in the media. It’s a great word to go along with a great service, and in the end, I’m just happy we’re not calling each other twits!
  I dug around through my Twitter archive and came up with some interesting tweets that show how and when the first Twitter client came to be.
 The first notion that Twitter didn’t belong solely on the web happened on December 5th, 2006 (a couple of weeks before the first check-in in the Twitterrific repository.) This line of thinking was inspired by a tweet John Gruber had posted about the  Dashboard widget he was using:
   is thinking that a Twitter UI belongs on the desktop, not in a widget — it's all about notification which you can't see unless you're F12'ed
   (Note that in the early days of Twitter, the website was designed around  @jack’s original idea of posting status messages. The user name was implied in the post, hence “Craig Hockenberry” “is experimenting”.)
 By the 20th of December, I felt confident enough in the state of the project to go public (that’s also the day that the prototype code got checked into the repository):
  At the Iconfactory, everyone gets a two week Christmas break. I’d rather code than shop :-)
  Unlike many products, we knew the name of what we were building very early on:
    Here’s the first tweet with a source parameter (that tells Twitter which client posted the tweet.) We worked with Twitter engineering to implement this, and for a long time Twitterrific was the only source besides “web”. Note that this happened just two days before the release!
